posts.text:
"We want to hire top 0.1% engineers"

I'm hearing this a lot right now. 

But what does that actually mean any more?

Back in 2015 that was simple, it meant they had a Computer Science degree from a Top 10 global university, they had done internships at Meta / Palantir / Google / Slack / Dropbox etc  during their degree, they'd competed in Olympiads and that they worked at high bar tech company, FAANG / HFT / T1 Start ups etc. 

They could fizz buzz in their sleep, wrote clean performant code and could knock up a whiteboard design of Slack from first principles with minimal prompts.

I think it's different now.

When I’m speaking to founders they’re looking for things you can’t see on paper

🤝They want ownership
 🏃They want hustle
🧘They want low ego
😗👌They want product taste

They want to see entrepreneurial energy, they want to see engineers that vibe code side hustles on the weekend, that are prepared to throw themselves into something.  

The problem is, most companies don't know these abilities even when they're sitting in their inbox. The orthodoxy of hiring misses them all together.

This is the problem we are spending every moment obsessing over here Dex

Link in the comments to sign up...

comments.text:
For most companies that basically means… “we’ll target people from certain employers because we assume they MUST be good if they work there…” … which is complete rubbish. Someone who’s incredible at Meta isn’t suddenly going to great in your org. 

Most employers arent actually that clear on what good looks like “here” in the context of their culture and ways of work… because they dont actually measure and manage that properly in the first place

resharedPost.text:
Graph Cheat Sheet - Union-Find


Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) is a very elegant data structure used in many graph algorithms. The below animation shows how it can be used to detect cycles within a graph.


𝐈𝐦𝐩𝐥𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧

1. Initialize each element as its own parent.

2. Find the root parent of a given node.

3. Union two sets of nodes by connecting their root parents

4. (Optional) Use Path Compression and Union by Rank to optimize performance.

𝐔𝐬𝐞 𝐂𝐚𝐬𝐞𝐬

1. Cycle Detection in a Graph

https://lnkd.in/e4we3nyY

2. Kruskal’s Algorithm for Minimum Spanning Tree

https://lnkd.in/eBKeGNGz

3. Count Connected Components in a Graph

https://lnkd.in/eTX36jpC


𝐓𝐢𝐦𝐞 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐒𝐩𝐚𝐜𝐞

Time: Near O(1) for both find and union operations due to path compression and union by rank.

Space: O(n) where n is the number of elements.


This animation is from the Advanced Algorithms course on neetcode.io and have been edited to fit on linkedin.


#dsa #unionfind #codinginterview

comments.text: This is such a fascinating data structure! It has a wide range of applications from finding connected components in a graph, to merging accounts, and finding minimum spanning trees in weighted graphs! The ability to find the parent of an item in constant time thanks to path compression makes this the ultimate tool for some tricky leetcode problems. A reminder that even a small change in algorithm design, or a different choice of data structure can have a profound impact on performance.

document.title: Intel AMX Docs for SimSIMD Contributors

posts.text:
Most modern AI boils down to matrix multiplications. Yet, fewer than 100 people worldwide have the practical experience to implement them efficiently across multiple platforms.

Since 2018, I've been working with tiled Matrix Multiplication instructions, starting with NVIDIA Titan Volta GPUs. It's frustrating to see how little things have improved. With custom instructions from CPU vendors — Intel Corporation AMX, Apple AMX, Arm SME, and more — each comes with its own complexities, making performance optimization harder, not easier.

It seems like CPU vendors are competing to create the most obscure Instruction Set Architecture (ISA) rather than focusing on Tensor Operations Per Second (TOPS). Take Intel AMX, for example — its 25-page documentation lacks details, like CPU support, compiler support, or even instruction latency/throughput!

At times, performance-oriented programming feels like hacking and reverse engineering!

---

If you're up for the challenge, check out the AMX manual and consider contributing to SimSIMD (https://lnkd.in/d9M9T_cH).

comments.text: Do you think a language like Mojo is a step in the right direction to solve this problem?

resharedPost.text:
Eric Schmidt, former Google CEO, explains why Google fumbled its lead in AI: 

- Google prioritized work-life balance over winning
- Startups succeed because people "work like hell"
- Founders are special and need to be in charge
- Time matters more in tech due to network effects

Here's the kicker:

While Google employees went home early...
Their competitors were pulling all-nighters.

Schmidt's take on Elon Musk?

"As much as we can dislike Elon's personal behavior, look at what he gets out of people."

Now, this isn't about glorifying burnout.

It's about understanding the battlefield.

But what do you think?

—

#VenchaVideosforFounders | Video #17

Find us at Vencha  💛

Read about my Book - The Go To Market Handbook for SaaS Leaders here -
https://buff.ly/47mUhM4

comments.text:
Sorry, but Google fumbled their lead in AI due to bad strategy. Strategy set straight at the top. Google had Deep Mind for over a decade and did nothing with it. When internal people would ask about it we were told that the AI strategy was to silently improve things with AI in the background.
Once OpenAI went public with GPT 3 they got scared and changed to "publicly launch half-baked crap with AI everywhere"
Don't blame your smart, capable, well-adjusted people for your bad strategy.
Not only because it's morally indefensible but because if you do you cannot correct your mistake.
I wouldn't have thought Eric Schmidt of all people would need to hear that.

author.occupation: Co-Founder Grounded AI

comments.text:
Sounds awfully like getting correlation and causation confused. I’m going to go out in a limb and suggest that firms like Credit Suisse, Dewey and Lebouef, Bear Stearns and Lehman Brothers all had staff who routinely pulled all nighters… oh look! Anyone can do this.

Business history is absolutely littered with companies that had an advantage in a field and squandered it: Nokia, Kodak etc.  Bill Gates thought Psion was Microsoft’s biggest competitor… where did Psion go? 

None of that was down to working patterns. It was down to choices made by leaders. It was competitors seeing an opportunity and moving quickly.

If you’d been asked to pick winners in the mid-1990s as to who would dominate the internet you’d have been wildly wrong… my guess is the winners of the AI era don’t even exist yet. Worth noting that there are plenty of reasons to be concerned about choices some AI businesses are making regarding ethics. If you are a business ‘constrained’ by that then you’ll make different choices to those who aren’t.

Full disclosure: I work in the office every day!

author.occupation: Head of ESG at Wood Mackenzie

comments.text:
let me tell you a story so 20+ odd years ago a company attracted some incredible minds, acquired startups with incredible people, initially it was all very exciting and they made great moves, people stayed late because they truly LOVED what they did. 

BUT then lots of processes came in blocking lots of creativity and freedom to be who they where hired to be - good people started to leave. 
To stop that the company then made everyone very, very, very  comfortable instead of unblocking those real issues. Double salaries, crazy shares, crazy benefits, an easy life...

BUT they did not provide the very things/ purpose that spark those brilliant minds. 
Some got out and went on to brilliant things BUT some got used to earning 500k+ more, got trapped by big mortgages etc and couldn't find other roles to match that money, so they stayed - not for the love of the work. 
Going home early because what they loved to do wasnt available anymore - nothing sparking minds so much they lost the need to want to leave as early as possible.
BUT when you take a brilliant mind and squash it a little each day you eventually bury the spark and passion. 
You don't force people to work harder / later you give them a purpose so strong it lights that spark

author.occupation: Leading Tech Execs Into the Future. | Host of The Secrets of Tech Outliers Podcast | Stealth AI Start-Up Coming Soon

comments.text:
People burn out when they cant deliver on the mission because of constraints.  There are 3 rules to follow:

1. TMI5 - Tell me like I'm 5 - this is how to clarify the mission.
2. YAGNI - You Aint Gonna Need It.  Get rid of any overheads or cumbersome processes.
3. DIMI - Do It like you Mean It - passion first.

These drive innovation, transformation and success.

author.occupation: Chief Technology Officer, Private Equity Operator and Advisor, Global CIO 200 award.

comments.text:
agree with everything... but

A burnt out brain is no brain at all. Get your staff ready for a campaign, a season, a workstream, a movement and dominate this. Then mindfully change seasons to reflect and perfect what you conquered.

I have an allergic reaction to the 9, 9, 6 culture. To be inspired by purpose is amazing, but to abandon your family, your religion, your friends shows limited wisdom.

All empires eventually topple over. They fail because they couldn't deal with seasons - they were highly geared for one season. When that season was over, they failed to recognise it and failed to transition...

author.occupation: CIO: Capitec Bank | 7 x AWS Certified

comments.text:
I personally believe in a „simple rule“: if you want to achieve great things that are very hard, you need to put in more effort than the average. 
Things are never 100% clear before you start. 
You’ll make mistakes and young companies are also inefficient. It is not possible to achieve great things with a 9-18 attitude. Simply impossible. 

What is not sustainable in my view is that this happens continuously based on top-down „pressure“. You need to bring a core group of people in your team that are intrinsically motivated and connected to the company’s purpose. Those people want to personally contribute, they want to solve challenges, they want to play with other A-players. Then top-down targets, a clear purpose and A-players are key 💪🏼

author.occupation: CEO & Co-Founder MakerVerse | Helping Procurement & Engineering Teams Stop Delays, Cut Costs & Get Manufacturing Parts On Time | ex-VP Additive Siemens

comments.text:
Classic case of confusing cause and effect. Google hasn't released an innovative product in over decade since it became a marketing and financial machine - which is fine - all mature companies do this. Its HR policies reflect that. Shareholders buy the stock precisely because of that. That is why Sundar still has a job. 
Just don't expect it to be a startup or act like one. In this context Gemini isn't a failure, it's just a boring, undifferentiated, me-too but good-enough product. 
There is no need for anyone to pull all-nighters and sleep on the server room floors for that.

author.occupation: CEO & Co-founder at datascalehr | Instant Payroll Connectivity Worldwide

comments.text: If a leader's only advice is to "work harder" then they should be the first to get replaced by AI. Just shows the tech boom is over, if we're out of inspiration, and only have perspiration left.

author.occupation: Shit poster in the streets, vibe coder in the sheets.

comments.text: Know the rules of engagement, if you want to play big games, understand what the risks are. We are so much more capable than we ever imagine, doing hard things is good for us. Just make sure you read the contract first.

author.occupation: ImpactOS & myday Founder

comments.text:
Eric Schmidt and many Valley execs are conveniently placing blame on employees, their work ethics and justifying layoffs with it. Except those employees don’t run the company. People like him did or do. And they all have an Office of the CEO and executive team whose entire job is to decide on company priorities, OKRs, watch market trends and keep their company competitive. They get paid 10x more than the employees, who build to those goals.
 Gen AI tools including OpenAI and ChatGPT were built partly by ex big tech or ex / Google employees. Which tells us that they had to leave to build it! This sweeping statement is also typical of someone at the top, who doesn’t know employees day to day. Not everyone has work life balance. Many of Google’s global eg Asian and Indian teams overwork, as that’s the work culture and they have less resources and have to often do meetings at U.S. time ( their night). Look at Google role ratios too: disproportionately high # of Eng - who then say they’ve great work life balance ( naturally!) while there’s 2UX or Marketing to every 13 Eng - and they’re consistently reporting burnt out. Big tech claim theyre all AI + algo and data driven- yet make sweeping statements like this with 0 data.

resharedPost.text:
A new IndabaXEgypt webinar coming next week 📢📢
 
When: Saturday 27th March, 5pm Cairo time
Title: "Hardware Support for AI and Vice Versa"
Speaker: Prof.Mohamed Zahran
Registration link: https://cutt.ly/indbx-web6

This talk is unique as it will be the first IndabaXEgypt webinar around hardware support for AI applications and we are we lucky to have Prof. Mohamed Zahran to explore this space with us. Mohamed Zahran is a professor with the Computer Science Department, Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences at New York University (NYU).

About the Talk:
As AI, especially ML, became a buzzword about a decade ago, a lot of research, both in industry and academia emerged to build better hardware to support it. In this talk, we will discuss several issues regarding AI and hardware design. We will try to answer the following questions. Why do we need specialized hardware for AI? What does it mean "better" hardware for AI? Can AI support hardware beside the traditional computer-aided-design tools? How will AI evolve and how will this affect the hardware? During the talk, we will raise questions as much as we give answers.

 #machinelearning #ai #artificialintelligence #deeplearning

resharedPost.text:
Janet Yellen, who has just been nominated by President-Elect Joe Biden for Secretary of the Treasury, received an honorary Doctor of Commercial Science degree from President John Sexton during NYU's 2014 Commencement ceremony at Yankee Stadium. In her address to that year's graduating class, she spoke about the importance of grit and having the courage to take a stand:

"There is an unfortunate myth that success is mainly determined by something called 'ability.' But research indicates that our best measures of these qualities are unreliable predictors of performance in academics or employment. Psychologist Angela Lee Duckworth says that what really matters is a quality she calls 'grit'—an abiding commitment to work hard toward long-range goals and to persevere through the setbacks that come along the way.

"One aspect of grit that I think is particularly important is the willingness to take a stand when circumstances demand it. Such circumstances may not be all that frequent, but in every life, there will be crucial moments when having the courage to stand up for what you believe will be immensely important."

Photo credit: ©Gallo: Courtesy of NYU Photo Bureau.

Video of the full speech: http://spr.ly/6043HKOnh
